coredata.dc:title: Surface amino group modulation of carbon dots with blue, green and red emission as Cu2+ ion reversible detector
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qingling
Li, Xiang
Wang, Xiaotong
Zang, Zehao
Liu, Heyan
Li, Lanlan
Yu, Xiaofei
Yang, Xiaojing
Lu, Zunming
Zhang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: The surface functional groups of carbon dots (CDs) play a significant role in the luminescence performances, but it is still unclear about the effects of special groups on the luminescence mechanism since there are different groups on the surface of prepared CDs. Here, electrochemical method was adopted to synthesize initial CDs and graphite rods served as raw materials, and initial CDs were only modified with amino groups to regulate their surface states, which lead to the blue, green and red luminescence for the modified CDs. The amino groups with different contents are only introduced on the surface of CDs without affecting carbon core. With the increased contents of amino groups, the luminescence spectra of CDs can be successfully modulated from blue to red emission. In addition, the blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) have a specific response to Cu2+ ions and can be applied as a fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ ions in the solution. Moreover, the addition of DL-serine can restore the blue emission of B-CDs quenched by Cu2+ ions. Therefore, the B-CDs have great potential application as a fluorescent reversible ion detector.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Surface state modulation
Photoluminescence mechanism
Ion detection
Fluorescent probes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Schematic structure and energy levels of prepared CDs
Detection of Cu2+ ions by B-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Display Materials and Photoelectric Devices
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51771068, 52001108, 51871088), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. E2021202022, B2021202011), and Key Laboratory of Display Materials and Photoelectric Devices, Ministry of Education.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CDs with diverse surface states using electrochemical method.
TEM images and size distribution (inset) of (a) I-CDs, (b) B-CDs, (c) G-CDs, and (d) R-CDs, respectively.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (c) emission spectra and (d) decay curves of I-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively.
(a) Full survey XPS spectra, (b) C 1 s and (c) O 1 s spectra with fitting results for I-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, (d) N 1 s spectra and fitting curves of B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs.
The schematic structure of prepared CDs with multiple emissions and energy level diagram of oxygen or nitrogen-related surface states for CDs.

(a) Histogram of fluorescence intensity change for B-CDs after adding different metal ions (50 μM), (b) emission spectra of the B-CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0–300 μM), The inset picture gives the image of the B-CDs (left) and the Cu2+-B-CDs complex (right) in water under 365 nm irradiation, the inset curve shows the linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and [Cu2+] as [Cu2+] = 20–120 μM, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (d) fluorescence decay curves of B-CDs with various concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0–300 μM).

(a) Emission and (b) excitation spectra of Cu2+-B-CDs solution after adding various concentrations of DL-serine (0–20 mg/mL), (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (d) decay curves of Cu2+-B-CDs solution after adding different concentrations of DL-serine (0–10 mg/mL), respectively.
The element contents of I-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Surface amino group modulation of carbon dots with blue, green and red emission as Cu2+ ion reversible detector

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clarifying the effects of surface amino groups on the luminescence and electronic structure for CDs.

The surface functional groups of carbon dots (CDs) play a significant role in the luminescence performances, but it is still unclear about the effects of special groups on the luminescence mechanism since there are different groups on the surface of prepared CDs. Here, electrochemical method was adopted to synthesize initial CDs and graphite rods served as raw materials, and initial CDs were only modified with amino groups to regulate their surface states, which lead to the blue, green and red luminescence for the modified CDs. The amino groups with different contents are only introduced on the surface of CDs without affecting carbon core. With the increased contents of amino groups, the luminescence spectra of CDs can be successfully modulated from blue to red emission. In addition, the blue emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) have a specific response to Cu2+ ions and can be applied as a fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ ions in the solution. Moreover, the addition of DL-serine can restore the blue emission of B-CDs quenched by Cu2+ ions. Therefore, the B-CDs have great potential application as a fluorescent reversible ion detector.

ce:section.ce:para: As a rising star of carbon related nanomaterials, carbon dots have attracted much attention since they have diverse and excellent properties [1–3]. The carbon dots have low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost, tunable photoluminescence spectra, good water or oil solubility, high quantum efficiency and good stability [4,5]. Therefore, CDs have great potential applications in various fields such as detection [6], illumination [7], imaging [8] and catalysis [9]. Since CDs were first synthesized in 2004 [10], the synthesis methods have been explored and developed. The synthetic strategies are mainly divided into two categories, one type is top-down methods, and the other type is bottom-up methods [11]. The top-down synthetic routes for preparing CDs possess higher yields, and the surface of CDs is relatively pure, but the luminescence spectrum is narrow and the performance is relatively simple. CDs prepared with the bottom-up methods have rich surface functional groups as well as excellent and diverse performances, but own low yield and high energy consumption. According to the current research on the luminescence mechanism of CDs, the photoluminescence properties of CDs are mainly depended upon quantum size effect, surface states, and elements doping [12]. The surface groups can modulate the spectral properties of CDs, but it is difficult to disclose the luminescence mechanism of CDs with different kinds of surface functional groups. The most common oxygen functional groups including C − O, C = O and COO − can regulate the spectra and quantum yield of CDs. Ding et al. obtained full-color luminescence CDs through hydrothermal synthesis and chromatographic separation, and the various oxidation degrees led to the different emitting wavelengths of CDs [13]. Furthermore, the surface oxidation degree also affects the energy gap of CDs, and it is mainly caused by the coupling of π electron system with carbonyl groups [14–16]. Qin et al. synthesized carbon dots by electrochemical etching of graphite rods, and introduced multiple emission centers by regulating the oxygen related functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, thus realizing the luminescence regulation of CDs [17].

Among the plenty of surface functional groups, nitrogen related groups have tremendous influence upon the spectral performances of CDs. Sun et al. utilized the hydrothermal method to prepare CDs with chitosan as raw material, and the excellent photoluminescence properties of CDs were induced by the amides formed on the surface and the incorporation of nitrogen atoms [18]. Pathak et al. found that the pyridine nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen on the surface of N-CDs were related to electron non-radiative recombination and radiation recombination [19]. However, when nitrogen related compounds are involved in the synthesis of CDs, in addition to forming new N-related surface states on CDs, some nitrogen atoms will enter the graphite carbon nucleus to form graphitized nitrogen, pyridine nitrogen, or pyrrole nitrogen, and their specific effects are difficult to distinguish. Qian et al. synthesized a series of N-containing fluorescent CDs, and it was discovered that amino and pyrrole N could produce more protolysis or aromatic structures to promote radiation recombination, thus improving the luminescence performance [20]. Up to now, there are some reports on the regulation of surface states, but it is difficult to achieve single surface state regulation for most preparation methods. Therefore, it is significant to clarify the effects of single surface group on the luminescence performances of CDs, which is helpful to disclose the surface state modulation mechanism for CDs. Nitrogen-containing functional groups with lone pair electrons contribute to the n-electron orbitals in modified CDs [21]. It has been reported that the amino groups could lead to red shifts and maintain the strong luminescence intensity of CDs in theoretical study [22]. In addition, the electron-donating amino groups can affect the emission wavelength of the CDs by charge transfer via computational methods [23].

In order to ascertain the influence mechanism of surface states, a single functional group was introduced on the surface of CDs. Here we used organic compounds only containing amino groups as additives during the preparation of CDs with electrochemical method. Compared with other methods such as hydrothermal method, the electrochemical method avoids the introduction of nitrogen atoms into the carbon nucleus skeleton, but only introduces the amino groups on the surface of the CDs. Interestingly, multiple emissions have been achieved from blue to red luminescence by the surface amino groups of CDs, and the role of surface amino groups has been clarified. In addition, it is found that the blue emitting CDs have a specific fluorescence quenching response to Cu2+ ions and the fluorescence can be recovered by DL-serine, Therefore, the B-CDs can be used as a reversible fluorescent probe for Cu2+ ions.

O-phenylenediamine was bought from Tianjin Jinke Fine Chemical Research Institute. P-phenylenediamine was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. 1, 8-Diaminonaphthalene was acquired from Macklin Company. NaCl was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Factory. Graphite rods were bought from Tianjin Zhongnuo Carbon Factory. DL-serine was purchased from Aladdin Company. The deionized water was used in the experiment. All chemicals were used without refinements.

In the preparation of CDs, two graphite rods were connected to a constant voltage power supply as the anode and cathode, and deionized water was the electrolyte, which was consistent with the reported method [19]. The CDs (I-CDs (Initial CDs), B-CDs (Blue CDs), G-CDs (Green CDs) and R-CDs (Red CDs)) with different surface states were prepared by the electrochemical method. Firstly, 100 mg NaCl was added to 50 mL deionized water to form a clear and transparent ionic solution. Secondly, two parallel graphite rods were inserted into the ionic solution as the anode and cathode with a distance of 1 cm. Static potentials in the range of 15–60 V were added on the two electrodes with a direct current power supply. After constant agitation for 12 h, the graphite rod of anode was etched, and a dark solution was formed gradually in the reactor. Finally, the CDs were obtained. I-CDs were prepared at a voltage of 18 V and initial current density of 0.822 mA/mm2 with a distance of 1 cm between two parallel graphite rods. B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized in the same way except for adding different surfactants during the preparation process. The mass of I-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs powders is 1.08 g, 1.42 g, 1.68 g and 1.69 g, respectively. B-CDs and G-CDs were synthesized with addition of 1 mmol 1, 8-diaminonaphthalene and o-phenylenediamine, respectively. R-CDs were prepared by adding a total of 1 mmol o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine with a ratio of 3:1.

Morphology of the prepared CDs was measured by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained with a spectrometer (Bruker, WQF-410). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken via a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi). The ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were performed on a spectrophotometer (Hitachi, U-3900H). The excitation and emission spectra, quantum yield and decay curves of prepared CDs were obtained with a steady and transient state spectrophotometer (Horiba, FL-3–22).

By adjusting the voltage and electrode spacing, it was found that the emission peak of I-CDs was red-shifted to longer wavelength as the voltage was increased and the electrode spacing became smaller (Figure S1). In addition to precursors and electrochemical parameters, the NaCl is the key to the formation of large-scale CDs in a short time. In the absence of NaCl, the preparation time of CDs is 120 h, which is much slower than the preparation time with addition of NaCl (12 h for I-CDs). This indicates that activation occurs during the anodic oxidation of NaCl, which promotes the rupture of chemical bonds in graphite, thus speeding up the preparation process of CDs [24]. In the electrochemical preparation of CDs, 1,8-diaminonaphthalene, p-phenylenediamine or o-phenylenediamine only containing amino groups were selected to adjust the surface states of CDs (Fig. 1 , Figure S2). The three compounds are similar in structure and they contain amino groups and conjugated aromatic rings. The formation process of I-CDs was speculated to be that the graphite rod was corroded and oxidized under electrochemical conditions, and thus dissociating into water-soluble nanoparticles [25]. When 1, 8-diaminaphthalene, o-phenylenediamine or p-phenylenediamine was added, the amino groups of the nitrogen containing compounds were combined on the surface of CDs in situ during the electrochemical reaction.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM images and particle size distribution of four types of CDs, and the CDs are quasi-spherical in shape. The prepared CDs have good dispersibility in aqueous solution. According to the particle size distribution (inset of Fig. 2), it can be seen that the average sizes of four types of CDs are between 1.0 nm and 2.0 nm. As a result, the change of luminescence performances for the four kinds of CDs should not be ascribed to the quantum size effect, but should be related to the surface states. In particular, according to the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the four kinds of CDs (Figure S3), there is no obvious lattice fringe can be well observed due to the ultrasmall sizes. In addition, the Raman peaks at 1354 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 are attributed to D and G bands [26]. The ratio of ID/IG represents the degree of graphitization of carbon materials [27,28]. Raman spectra (Figure S4) show that the ID/IG is 1.05, 1.04, 1.03 and 1.05 for I-CDs, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. It can be concluded that the graphitization of four CDs is almost identical, which confirms the modification does not affect the carbon nucleus, but only changes surface states of the CDs.

Surface groups of the four kinds of CDs are investigated by FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, the main difference between I-CDs and other three CDs is that I-CDs do not contain N related chemical bonds. B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs have the same kind of chemical bonds on the surface, including NH2 and O-H (3380 cm−1), C-H (2904 cm−1, 703 cm−1, 624 cm−1), C = O (1676 cm−1), C = N (1616 cm−1), and C-O (980 cm−1) bonds [6,29]. The FTIR spectra indicate that the three surface modulated CDs have the same type of chemical bonds, and the difference is the relative contents of chemical bonds. There is a trend that the ratio of oxygen-related chemical bonds to nitrogen related chemical bonds is the highest in B-CDs, followed by G-CDs, and the lowest in R-CDs. The results indicate that the oxidation of CDs is weakened, while the nitrogen contents are increased from B-CDs to R-CDs. Fig. 3b displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the four types of CDs. The four CDs have broad absorption peaks in the range of 200–300 nm and 340–530 nm, which are ascribed to π–π* electronic transition of aromatic C = C bonds, and the n-π* electronic transition of the aromatic sp2 system containing C = O or C-N bonds, respectively [30]. In addition, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs have absorption peaks centered at 330 nm, 420 nm and 517 nm, respectively [31]. This indicates that different electronic transitions are induced by the functional surface states [32,33]. Fig. 3c gives the emission spectra of four kinds of CDs. It shows that I-CDs own a wide emission peak centered at 550 nm, which is probably induced by the oxidation of carbon core [34]. B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs have optimal emission peaks centered at 450 nm, 558 nm and 608 nm, respectively. The blue, green and red emission can be realized by the modulation of amino groups on the surface of I-CDs. When 1,8-Diaminonaphthalene with two amino groups was introduced to the I-CDs, the B-CDs have only one emission peak located at 450 nm under various excitation wavelengths (Figure S5). Moreover, the optimal excitation peaks of I-CDs and B-CDs are centered at 365 nm, and the optimal excitation peaks for G-CDs and R-CDs are around 380 nm (Figure S6a). In addition, I-CDs were also modulated by adding 0.25 mmol, 0.5 mmol 1, 8-diaminaphthalene. And compared with I-CDs prepared with adding 1 mmol 1, 8-diaminaphthalene, it is found that the optimal emission peaks are all around 450 nm under 365 nm excitation (Figure S6b). As o-phenylenediamine with two amino groups is utilized to regulate the surface states of I-CDs, it is noticed that the emission peak of G-CDs is red-shifted to 558 nm. Similarly, when the addition amount of o-phenylenediamine is 0.25 mmol and 0.5 mmol, the optimal emission peaks are still around 558 nm at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Figure S6c). The results suggest that the change of the amounts of amino additives has no linear correlation with the red shift of I-CDs. 1, 8-diaminaphthalene and o-phenylenediamine contain the same amount of amino group, but the chemical environment of the amino group is different. The lone pair electron on the amino group is paired with the π electron on the benzene ring, and the electron density on the nitrogen atom is reduced more, resulting in the N-H chemical bond in o-phenylenediamine is relatively weaker, more likely to break and react. It is supposed that the red shift of emission spectra is originated from the increased contents of amino groups on the surface of CDs. Therefore, p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine with more amino groups are selected to regulate the surface states of R-CDs. R-CDs have an optimal emission peak at 608 nm with a molar ratio of p-phenylenediamine to o-phenylenediamine of 1:3. In addition, we have explored the influence of the ratio of p-phenylenediamine to o-phenylenediamine on the fluorescence spectra, and it is found that all modulated R-CDs are in the red emission range with the increased ratio, and the fluorescence intensity is the highest when the ratio of p-phenylenediamine to o-phenylenediamine is 1:3 (Figures S7c and S7d). Similarly, as the total amounts of o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine are changed during the synthesis of R-CDs, it is found that the emission peak is almost fixed on the same excitation wavelength (Figure S6d). Moreover, only p-phenylenediamine was also introduced into the electrochemical preparation process for I-CDs, and it was found that only blue light emission could be achieved. The optimal emission and excitation peaks are located at 480 nm and 370 nm, respectively (Figure S7a and S7b). The substituent effect has been investigated in the substituted benzene to understand the interactions between materials [35,36]. It has also been reported the differences in physical and chemical interactions caused by different substituent positions of benzene rings with the same substituent [37–39]. As isomers of o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine, different positions of amino substituents lead to different surface bindings with CDs. When only o-phenylenediamine or p-phenylenediamine is used for surface regulation, the position of fluorescence emission peak cannot be changed with increased amount, indicating that the amount introduced into CDs surface is limited (Figure S6c and Figure S7a). According to XPS results, nitrogen content was increased when o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine were co-introduced into the surface of CDs (Table S1), which led to the red emission of the modified CDs. Therefore, it is found that diverse surfactants only containing amino groups can control the blue-shift or red-shift of the peak position of I-CDs. Since the modulated CDs only change with amino groups, the main difference is originated from the relative contents of amino groups, which results in the change of emission spectra. More importantly, B-CDs show good stability under different concentrations of NaCl (0–1000 μM), wide pH range (4–12), UV irradiation for 40 min and different temperatures (20–50 °C) (Figure S8).

Fig. 3d gives the decay curves of four kinds of CDs. The average fluorescence lifetime of I-CDs is 209.84 ns, which is different from the average lifetimes of other three CDs (Table S2). The fluorescence of CDs mostly occurs on timescales in the order of nanoseconds to tens of nanoseconds in other reports [40–43]. It has been reported the increasing degree of oxidation attributed to surface oxygen functional groups or oxidative defects enables the tuning of ultralong lifetimes from room-temperature phosphorescence to thermally activated delayed fluorescence by controlling singlet–triplet energy splitting in graphene quantum dots [44]. It is speculated that the fluorescence lifetime of I-CDs is significantly higher than that of the amino-modified CDs, which may be caused by the higher oxidation degree of I-CDs. According to research on the effects of surface oxidation degree on the luminescent CDs in electrochemical methods, the increase of applied potentials could improve the oxidation degree of CDs [45]. The oxidation degree of I-CDs was regulated by changing the applied potentials. The fluorescence decay curves of CDs prepared with different voltages are shown in the Figure S9a. With the increase of applied potentials, the fluorescence lifetimes are gradually increased (Table S3). As shown in the Figure S9b, the oxidation degree of CDs can be also regulated with different contents of oxidants. It is found that the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs are also increased with the increase of the addition of (NH4)2S2O8 (Table S4). It may explain the significantly higher fluorescence lifetime of I-CDs than amino-modified CDs due to the higher degree of surface oxidation in I-CDs. It is assumed that the various lifetimes are induced by the change of the electron transition process. In addition, the decay rate of surface modulated CDs is gradually increased, and the average lifetimes are 5.00 ns, 2.05 ns, and 2.02 ns for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. This indicates that the time of electrons staying in the excited state becomes shorter, and more electrons are returned to the ground states in the form of non-radiative transitions [7,14,46]. In addition, the quantum efficiency of I-CDs is only 0.07%, and the quantum efficiency of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is 3.21%, 0.88% and 0.86% in water solution, respectively. The radiative and non-radiative rate constants of the four CDs were calculated using the formula of the reported paper [47], as shown in Table S5. It indicates that the surface amino groups can enhance the radiative transition and increase the luminescence efficiency [48]. The quantum efficiency of CDs is decreased from B-CDs to R-CDs, which may be induced by the increased ratio of non-radiative transitions to radiative transitions with increasing amino groups. The results once again confirm that the surface states of CDs can be successfully modulated by amino groups, which has great effects on the surface bonding and electronic energy levels of the I-CDs. The element contents and chemical bonding states of four CDs are also investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 . According to the full XPS spectra (Fig. 4a), I-CDs contain C, O elements, while B-CDs, G-CDs, R-CDs are consisted of C, O, N elements, which discloses that amino groups are successfully introduced for surface modulated CDs. In addition, the spectral modulation is related to the regulation of the amino groups [49].

Fine scan XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s for the prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 4b, 4c, 4d, respectively. The C 1 s spectra of I-CDs can be fitted well with three component peaks, corresponding to C = C/C-C (284.6 eV), C = O (288.4 eV) and C-O (286.1 eV) bonds. The C 1 s spectra of the three modified CDs contain four component peaks, which are corresponded to C = C/C-C (∼284.0 eV), C = O (∼288.0 eV), C-O (∼287.0 eV) and C-N (∼285.0 eV) bonds. It is worth noting that with the red shift of the optimal emission peak position from B-CDs to R-CDs, the contents of C-N bonds are increased gradually, while the contents of C = O and C-O bonds are decreased (Table S1 and Table S6), which indicates that the surface modification of amino groups is increased. The reason for this phenomenon is that oxygen related functional groups on the surface of CDs are replaced by the amino groups, which suggests that the contents of surface nitrogen have a significant effect on CDs. The O 1 s spectra of four types of CDs can be well fitted with two curves centered at 531.9 and 535.9 eV, corresponding to C = O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4c) [50]. Moreover, the N 1 s spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs have only one component peak at 399.0 eV, which is ascribed to the amino groups. This is consistent with the FTIR spectra, which confirms that the three modified CDs are rich in nitrogen-containing and oxygen related groups.

The element contents of the four kinds of CDs are given in Table 1 . Here, B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs contain similar surface states with amino groups. Obviously, I-CDs are unmodified CDs and do not contain nitrogen elements. The nitrogen contents in B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs are increased gradually, while the oxygen contents are decreased. The element content analysis indicates that the red shift of modified CDs is correlated to the higher nitrogen content, and the increase of amino groups is conducive to the red shift of emission peaks for modified CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Schematic structure and energy levels of prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence mechanism of amino groups modulated multi-color fluorescent CDs is explored. According to TEM images, the particle size distribution of four types of CDs is similar and in the range of 1–2 nm. Therefore, the difference of fluorescence spectra should not be induced by the quantum size effect [24,25,29]. Moreover, the XPS, FTIR and Raman spectra confirm the N elements are not incorporated into the carbon core of the I-CDs, but only modulated on the surface of CDs. Therefore, the luminescence mechanism of modified CDs is mainly originated from the surface states. A schematic structure and energy level diagram of the four CDs are given to explain the luminescence mechanism, as shown in Fig. 5 . The prepared CDs are composed of carbon core and surface states. For I-CDs, there are only C = O and OH groups on the surface. The electrons in I-CDs are excited from the ground states to the excited states, after electrons relaxation to the lower excited states, then the excited electrons transfer to oxygen related surface state levels and emit green light. The surface oxidation states associated with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups emit green light with energy level gap of about 2.3 eV for I-CDs. After the addition of amino compounds in the electrochemical reaction, the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on I-CDs become the active sites of the reaction, and the amino nitrogen replaces the position of oxygen, so as to form a new luminescence center on the amino modulated CDs. B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs have similar surface structures, and only amino groups are introduced on the surface besides hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. The surface energy level gap for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs is 2.8 eV, 2.2 eV and 2.0 eV, respectively. It is reasonably deduced that emission centers on CDs change from oxygen-related energy states to nitrogen-related energy states.

It is worthy to note that nitrogen-related functional groups with lone pair electrons introduce n-electron orbitals which raise the HOMO level and narrow the energy level gap of modified CDs, resulting in the redshift of emission as previously reported [21]. In addition, the increased number of amino groups as electron donor groups in CDs would correspondingly enhance the charge density, thus gradually narrowing the energy level gap [51]. The XPS and FTIR results indicate that the amino contents are increased from B-CDs to R-CDs, which leads to the decrease of energy level gap and red shift of photoluminescence for amino group modified CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ ions by B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As a heavy metal element, copper ions have a certain degree of pollution to the environment, and excessive Cu2+ ions will result in the poison and damage of the organism [52]. Therefore, the detection of Cu2+ ions is necessary to ensure the health of the environment and organisms. Compared to G-CDs and R-CDs, B-CDs have higher photoluminescence intensity and good water solubility, which can be applied to Cu2+ ions detection. In order to confirm the selectivity of the Cu2+ ions, a series of ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, K+, Ba2+, Cr3+ ions) were detected by B-CDs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is significantly decreased by Cu2+ ions, but other ions have no obvious effect on the luminescence intensity of B-CDs. On the one hand, nitrogen-containing or oxygen-containing functional groups have good binding ability with Cu2+ ions [53]; on the other hand, it is reported that Cu2+ ions have stronger binding ability to N compared with other ions [54]. Furthermore, Cu2+ ions still have good quenching effect on B-CDs even though other ions co-exist in the system, as shown in the Figure S10. This means that B-CDs own good selectivity to Cu2+ ions, and it has great potential as a Cu2+ ion detector. In addition, the effect of Cu2+ ions with different concentrations on the photoluminescence properties of B-CDs was tested, as shown in Fig. 6b. Obviously, as the concentration of Cu2+ ions is increased, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is gradually reduced, and finally the emission of B-CDs is wholly depressed (Figure S11). The inset of Fig. 6b shows that B-CDs present a bright blue light under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and the fluorescence is obviously extinguished after adding Cu2+ ions. The inset curves display a well linear relationship (R2 = 0.9901) between (F0-F)/F0 of B-CDs and [Cu2+] as the concentration of Cu2+ ions is in the range of 0–120 μM. The fitting equation is shown as follows:
(1)
( F
0
- F ) /
F 0
= 0.00092
[ C ]
+ 0.02226
R
2
= 0.9901

Where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs and Cu2+-B-CDs, [C] is the concentration of Cu2+ ions. Based on the S/N = 3 principle, the lowest detection limit of B-CDs for Cu2+ ions was calculated to be 0.16 μM. In addition, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was explored. As shown in Fig. 6c, the UV–Vis absorption spectra show the change of the absorption peak of B-CDs with different concentrations of Cu2+ ions. Obviously, it is able to see that the intensity of the UV–Vis absorption peak at 330 nm decreases with the increased concentration of Cu2+ ions. It is consistent with the gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity for B-CDs as the concentration of Cu2+ ions is increased. This indicates that Cu2+ ions and B-CDs may form a complex by forming coordination bonds through the amino-containing groups on the surface. As shown in Fig. 6d and Table S7, the fluorescence decay curves display that the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs have slightly changed in the range of 6.22–9.85 ns with adding different concentrations of Cu2+ ions, which discloses that the quenching mechanism is originated from the static quenching [55–57]. In addition, the quenching mechanism is further determined according to the conventional Stern-Volmer equation as follows:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
sv
Q

Where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs and Cu2+-B-CDs, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and (Q) is the concentration of Cu2+ ions. The quenching mechanism is usually determined by the change of the system caused by the temperature. For static quenching, new substances are generated and the stability plays a leading role in the system, so the quenching constant decreases with the increase of temperature. The Stern-Volmer curves and equations at different temperatures (293, 298 and 303 K) are shown in Figure S12. As can be clearly observed from the Figure S12, the KSV decreases from 2.88 × 104 M−1 to 2.06 × 104 M−1 as the temperature increases from 293 to 303 K, which is exactly consistent with the static quenching mechanism. It is reasonable to speculate that the Cu2+ ions are reacted with the ground state molecules of B-CDs. During the quenching process, the ground state electrons are excited and entered the excited state, and the ground state orbital generates holes to form electron-hole pairs. Cu2+ ions can serve as an electron acceptor and block electrons from interacting with the holes, which leads to the fluorescence quenching [58,59].

In order to restore the fluorescence of B-CDs quenched by Cu2+ ions, DL-serine is selected to restore the fluorescence of Cu2+-B-CDs solution, while other substances such as L-Threonine, Tyrosine, EDTA, etc. have no ideal effect like DL-serine (Figure S13). DL-serine contains an alcoholic hydroxyl group and is more soluble in water than other substances. As the concentration of DL-serine is increased, the fluorescence intensity of the quenched B-CDs is gradually recovered, and the intensity of the optimal excitation peak also shows the same trend (Fig. 7 a-b). It has been reported that Cu2+ ions are easily bound to compounds with amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [60,61]. DL-serine is biologically important amino-acids which interact strongly with Cu2+ ions. It has been reported Cu2+ and DL-serine could form complexes by outer-sphere co-ordination between Cu2+ and un-ionized hydroxy-oxygen donor atoms in DL-serine [62]. It can be observed from the UV–Vis absorption spectra that the intensity of the absorption peak is gradually recovered (Fig. 7c), which indicates that DL-serine can remove Cu2+ ions from the Cu2+-B-CDs complex through a competitive reaction. In addition, the fluorescence decay rates and lifetimes (Fig. 7d, Table S8) are nearly unchanged with the addition of DL-serine, which is consistent with the lifetime of B-CDs quenched by Cu2+ ions. From this aspect, it can also explain the reason for the fluorescence intensity recovery. When the concentration of DL-serine is not less than 20 mg/mL, the fluorescence of B-CDs can recover to the ideal effect. B-CDs were applied to determination of Cu2+ in tap water and the North Canal River water. The recoveries of Cu2+ ions in tap water and the North Canal River water samples are 95.00–100.00% and 96.68–103.35%, respectively (Table S9). As shown in Table S10, the DL-serine recoveries of standard addition for tap water sample and the North Canal River water samples are 95.30–104.67% and 96.70–102.20%, respectively. Therefore, we have developed a fluorescent reversible detector for Cu2+ ions based on B-CDs, in the form of a turn-off–on switch, which has high selectivity and sensitivity.

In summary, surface amino groups modulated CDs with blue, green and red emission have been successfully synthesized with electrochemical method and the effects of amino groups on the optical properties for CDs have been systematically investigated. The different emissions of multi-color CDs can be achieved through the regulation of the surface amino groups. The effect of amino groups on the surface states for CDs is clarified, and the luminescence mechanism of surface amino groups is revealed. The enhancement of the amination degree makes the energy gap between the surface energy levels of the carbon dots narrow and presents polychromatic luminescence under light excitation. In addition, the luminescence of B-CDs can be quenched by the Cu2+ ions and recovered with addition of DL-serine, which can serve as a fluorescent reversible turn-off–on detector.

Qingling Zhao: Writing – original draft. Xiang Li: Data curation. Xiaotong Wang: Formal analysis. Zehao Zang: Investigation. Heyan Liu: Writing – review & editing. Lanlan Li: Resources. Xiaofei Yu: Validation. Xiaojing Yang: Visualization. Zunming Lu: Validation. Xinghua Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.153892.
